title: Quantum Grover Adaptive Search for Discrete Simulation Optimization
authors: Mingjie Hu, Jian-qiang Hu, Enlu Zhou
abstract: Quantum computing has advanced rapidly in recent years and has shown advantages in a variety of domains. In this paper, we investigate its potential for discrete simulation optimization in the fixed-confidence setting, a fundamental problem in the simulation literature. We first introduce a quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over all candidate solutions and provides the foundation for quantum algorithm design. Based on this oracle, we develop the first Grover-search-based quantum algorithm for discrete simulation optimization, called SOGAS. In particular, SOGAS uses a binary-search framework to progressively eliminate suboptimal solutions while carefully controlling the error probability, and eventually identifies a set of near-optimal solutions. We prove that SOGAS returns a near-optimal solution with probability at least the prescribed confidence level and achieves a quadratic speedup in the dependence of query complexity on the number of candidate solutions. Numerical experiments further show that SOGAS substantially outperforms classical benchmarks and provide empirical evidence for quantum advantage in discrete simulation optimization.
summary: This paper introduces SOGAS, a quantum algorithm designed to optimize stochastic, discrete simulations. By leveraging Grover-based techniques and quantum mean estimation, the authors demonstrate a quadratic speedup in finding near-optimal solutions compared to classical Monte Carlo methods. The work provides both theoretical guarantees for error control and numerical evidence of quantum advantage across various noise distributions.
main_problem: Achieving a quadratic speedup in the query complexity of discrete simulation optimization within a fixed-confidence (epsilon, delta-PAC) framework.
main_result: The developed SOGAS algorithm achieves a quadratic speedup in query complexity relative to the number of candidate solutions, reducing complexity from O(|X|) to O(sqrt(|X|/epsilon)).
method: The algorithm uses a binary-search-based framework (OptimalRegion) to narrow the performance interval, a quantum flagging algorithm (FlagOracle) to label near-optimal solutions, and amplitude amplification to extract the solution.
model_or_system: A quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over candidate solutions and encodes random noise and simulation outputs into a three-register quantum state (solution, noise, and output registers).
key_observables: The optimal mean performance y(x*) and the proportion of solutions meeting a specific performance threshold.
important_parameters: Optimality gap (epsilon), failure probability (delta), and the size of the discrete solution set (|X|).
main_assumptions: Assumes the existence of a quantum simulation oracle capable of coherent superposition and that performance values are bounded within [0, 1].
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Grover iteration mechanics; Figure 2 illustrates performance interval partitioning; Figure 3 depicts the binary search process; Figures 4-6 show empirical query complexity scaling and performance across different probability distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of discrete simulation optimization, defines a new quantum simulation oracle, presents the SOGAS algorithm (comprising subroutines for region identification, flagging, and amplification), provides a rigorous theoretical proof of complexity and correctness, and concludes with numerical validation using Qiskit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
